title: Fesenjan
subtitle: Persian lamb stew with walnuts and pomegranate molasses
excerpt: A Persian khoresh of lamb shoulder cooked low and slow in a thick, dark sauce of ground toasted walnuts and pomegranate molasses until the meat is tender and the sauce has reduced to a deep, bittersweet coating. One of the great stews of the Persian kitchen, complex, rich, and unlike anything else.
sourceNotes: Fesenjan is documented in Persian cookery manuscripts from the medieval period and appears in the sixteenth-century cookery accounts of the Safavid court. The combination of ground walnuts and sour fruit (originally sour pomegranate) is a defining flavour profile of northern Iranian (Gilaki) cuisine, particularly in the Gilan and Mazandaran provinces along the Caspian coast. The original version used duck or pheasant rather than lamb. Multiple cross-referenced Persian culinary sources.

content.text: Fesenjan is built on a technique that seems unlikely until you taste the result: ground toasted walnuts cooked in liquid for a long time, producing a thick, dark, slightly oily sauce with a deep nuttiness and a bitterness that balances the sweetness of the pomegranate molasses. The sauce should finish a very dark brown, nearly black in the most traditional versions. If it looks too dark, it is probably right.

Toast the walnuts properly before grinding them. Untoasted walnuts produce a paler, flatter sauce. The toasting takes 8 minutes in a dry pan and is not optional. The lamb can be replaced with bone-in chicken thighs or duck legs for a more traditional interpretation.

Toast the {{walnuts}} of walnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat for 7 to 8 minutes, stirring often, until fragrant and golden. Leave to cool, then grind in a food processor to a fine, crumbly paste. Set aside.
Brown the lamb and soften the onion

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a dutch oven over high heat. Season the {{lamb-shoulder}} of lamb with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and brown in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side. Set aside. Reduce the heat to medium, add the {{onion}} onion, and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{turmeric}} of turmeric and {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon and stir for 1 minute.

Return the lamb to the pot. Add the ground walnuts, {{pomegranate-molasses}} of pomegranate molasses, and {{stock-chicken}} of stock. Stir to combine, the sauce will be thick. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 1 hour until the lamb is tender. Remove the lid and cook for a further 15 minutes until the sauce has thickened and darkened. Taste and adjust the balance with a little more pomegranate molasses for sourness or a pinch of salt. Serve over basmati rice, scattered with {{pomegranate}} pomegranate seeds.

items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 15 to 20 minutes and cook over medium heat to reduce and thicken
items.cause: Lid kept on for the entire cook, or not enough reduction

items.fix: Toast walnuts properly before grinding; balance bitterness with a pinch of caster sugar or a drizzle of honey
items.cause: Walnuts not toasted, or too much pomegranate molasses

items.fix: The simmer should be very gentle, barely bubbling; cover and continue for another 30 minutes over low heat
items.cause: Heat too high, causing the meat to seize rather than braise

content.text: Fesenjan is a khoresh (a slow-cooked stew served with rice) from the Gilan and Mazandaran provinces of northern Iran, where walnut trees grow and pomegranates are cultivated along the Caspian coast. In Iran the sauce is cooked for a long time until very dark and concentrated; in diaspora recipes it is sometimes lighter and quicker. The darker version is the older version.
